title: Add coupon discount support to charge endpoint
summary: This PR introduces coupon-discount support but contains several serious bugs (discount_amount is always 0, negative charges are possible) and a critical PCI violation (card data logged to stdout). There are also silent error swallows that silently overcharge customers, unvalidated user input passed directly into business logic, production debug logs, and widespread naming/standards issues. The PR is not ready to merge.

findings.title: PCI violation: full request body including card data logged to stdout
findings.message: `console.log('DEBUG charge body:', JSON.stringify(theData))` serialises the entire raw request body — which contains card number, CVV, expiry, and any PAN-adjacent fields — and writes it to stdout. Any log aggregation system (Datadog, CloudWatch, Splunk, etc.) will ingest and index PII/PCI data, likely violating PCI DSS requirement 3.4. Remove this log line entirely. If you need observability, log only non-sensitive fields (customerId, currency, amount) after schema validation.

findings.title: `discount_amount` is always 0 — payload.amount mutated before the subtraction
findings.message: Line 24 overwrites `payload.amount` with `finalAmount` (the already-discounted value). Line 36 then computes `payload.amount - finalAmount`, which is `finalAmount - finalAmount = 0` for every request. The charge itself is sent correctly, but the response always reports zero discount regardless of the coupon applied. Fix: capture the original amount before the try block — `const originalAmount = payload.amount;` — and use `originalAmount - finalAmount` on line 36. Also remove the `payload.amount = finalAmount` mutation on line 24; it serves no purpose since `finalAmount` is already used for the charge call.

findings.title: Silently swallowed coupon error causes invisible overcharge
findings.message: The inner `try/catch` with a `// TODO` body swallows any exception thrown by `doStuff`. If an unexpected error occurs (e.g., a bug in a future coupon strategy), `finalAmount` stays at the original full price and the charge proceeds without any indication to the caller that the coupon was not applied. The customer is silently overcharged. Either propagate the error to `next(err)` or return a 422 with a meaningful message. At minimum, log the error.

findings.title: FixedCouponStrategy can produce a negative charge amount
findings.message: `return amount - this.amt;` has no floor guard. If a $3.00 order (300 cents) is charged with FIVEOFF (500 cents), the result is -200 — a negative charge. Downstream payment gateways typically reject negative amounts, which would throw an unhandled error; worse, some gateways interpret them as refunds. Add: `return Math.max(0, amount - this.amt);` and decide whether a zero-dollar charge should be allowed or should return a 422.

findings.title: couponCode read from raw body, bypasses schema validation entirely
findings.message: `couponCode` is extracted from `req.body` before `chargeSchema.parse()` runs and is never included in the schema. This means it receives no type-checking, no length limit, and no sanitisation — an attacker can send an arbitrarily long string. Add `couponCode: z.string().max(32).optional()` to `chargeSchema` and read the validated value from `payload` instead of `theData`.

findings.title: User-supplied coupon code logged verbatim — potential log-injection / PII leak
findings.message: `console.log('DEBUG: unknown coupon code:', couponCode)` writes attacker-controlled input directly to logs. This enables log-injection attacks and may also expose coupon codes that belong to other customers (e.g., if a customer shares a code from a different account). Replace with a structured warning at `warn` level that sanitises the value (e.g., truncate/redact), and remove the `DEBUG:` prefix since this will ship to production.

findings.title: `const response: any = transaction` discards all type safety
findings.message: Casting to `any` to tack on ad-hoc fields bypasses the TypeScript type system for the entire response object. Define a proper response type (or extend/intersect `Transaction`) that includes `discount_amount` and `coupon_code` as optional fields, and build a new object rather than mutating the one returned by the service layer.

findings.title: No unit tests for coupon math — explicitly called out as TODO
findings.message: The PR description marks unit tests as a follow-up TODO. The coupon arithmetic (percentage rounding with `Math.floor`, fixed discount floor-at-zero, unknown code handling) is exactly the logic that needs deterministic tests before merging, especially given that the discount_amount calculation is currently broken (see separate finding). Tests should be a merge requirement, not a follow-up, for payment-critical code.

findings.title: Exported function named `doStuff` — breaks discoverability and intent
findings.message: `doStuff` is the public API surface imported by the controller. Rename to `applyCouponDiscount(amount, couponCode)` or similar. Similarly, internal variable names `tmp`, `result2`, `x`, and the private field `p` should be renamed to `strategy`, `discountedAmount`, `discounted`, and `percentage` (or `rate`) respectively.

findings.title: `var` used instead of `const` — violates project/TS conventions
findings.message: Both `var x = ...` (line 17) and `var tmp = ...` (line 58) use `var`, which has function-scope semantics and is discouraged in modern TypeScript. Use `const` for both; neither is reassigned after declaration.

findings.title: Loose equality (`==`) used throughout — should be strict (`===`)
findings.message: All comparisons in `CouponHandlerFactory.build` use `==` instead of `===`. In TypeScript with `strict` mode this is a lint error and also conceptually wrong for string comparison. Replace all `==` with `===`.

findings.title: Long if-else chain should be a lookup map; `AbstractCouponStrategy` adds unnecessary ceremony
findings.message: The factory's if-else chain is harder to extend and review than a plain record/map. Replace with: `const COUPONS: Record<string, AbstractCouponStrategy> = { WELCOME10: new PercentageCouponStrategy(0.10), ... }` and `return COUPONS[code] ?? null`. Additionally, `AbstractCouponStrategy` adds a class hierarchy for what is essentially a single-method object; a plain `type CouponStrategy = { apply(amount: number): number }` or even a `Map<string, (amount: number) => number>` would be simpler and still extensible.

findings.title: Dead commented-out code should be removed
findings.message: The commented-out `FREESHIP` block adds noise with no value. If this coupon type is planned, track it in a ticket; remove it from the codebase.
